body:
I’ve sat with countless souls, their faces etched with the geography of sleepless nights and unresolved sorrows. They come to me with dreams that haunt them, dreams that feel like a relentless echo of their waking pain. And I tell them, your dreams are not the enemy. They are the fierce, loving surgeons of your soul, and their operating room is the sacred, often misunderstood, realm of REM sleep. We have been taught to fear the dark, to fear the unknown, to fear the depths of our own being. But I am here to tell you that the darkness is not empty. It is full of medicine. It is full of wisdom. It is full of the raw, untamed power of your own life force. As Kalesh, I have walked these shadowed lands of the soul for eons. I have seen the terror and the beauty that reside there. And I have learned that the only way out is through.

What is REM Sleep and Why Does It Matter?

Rapid Eye Movement (REM) sleep is one of the deepest stages of our nightly journey into the unconscious. It’s a paradoxical state where the body is largely paralyzed, yet the brain is as active as when we are awake. This is the stage where the most vivid and memorable dreams occur. But it’s not just a cinema of the mind. REM sleep is a critical biological process for memory consolidation, learning, and, most importantly, for emotional processing. It is the crucible in which the raw data of our experience is forged into the coherent narrative of our lives. Without it, we are adrift in a sea of unprocessed moments, unable to learn, unable to grow, unable to heal. Neurologically, this is a period of intense activity. The amygdala, the brain’s emotional center, is highly active, while the prefrontal cortex, the seat of logic and reason, takes a backseat. This allows for a free-flowing, associative, and highly symbolic processing of emotional material, unconstrained by the rigid logic of the waking mind.

Think of your mind as a library. Every day, you acquire new books – new experiences, new emotions, new information. During the day, these books are just piled up on the floor, disorganized and chaotic. It is during REM sleep that the librarian of your subconscious comes out to meticulously sort and shelve these books. Some are integrated into your main collection, some are archived, and some, the particularly painful or traumatic ones, are revisited, re-read, and re-contextualized until they lose their sharp edges. This is not a gentle process of dusting and tidying. It is a visceral, often violent, act of literary alchemy. It is the tearing out of pages, the burning of chapters, the rewriting of entire volumes. It is the soul’s fierce and uncompromising commitment to truth. And this librarian is not some meek, bespectacled scholar. This is a warrior-poet, a wild mystic who is not afraid to get their hands dirty.

I once sat with someone who had this exact dream every night for a month. When they finally understood what it meant, it stopped.

I am love, you are love, it is all love.

Stanislav Grof's research into non-ordinary states of consciousness suggests that these experiences are not pathological — they are developmental.

This is not a gentle or passive process. It is a dynamic and often turbulent one. The emotional intensity of our dreams is a direct reflection of the emotional intensity of our lives. When we suppress our feelings during the day, they don’t just disappear. They lie in wait, and in the darkness of REM sleep, they demand to be heard. And they will not be silenced. They will scream, they will rage, they will weep, they will do whatever it takes to get your attention. Your job is not to shut them up. Your job is to listen. To listen with your whole body, with your whole heart, with your whole being. To listen as if your life depends on it. Because it does.

The Dream’s Role in Healing

Your dreams are not random nonsense. They are a highly intelligent and deeply personal language of the soul. They are the raw, uncensored truth of your emotional landscape. And they are one of the most powerful tools you have for healing. But they are not a gentle lullaby. They are a primal scream. They are a declaration of war against the lies you have been telling yourself. They are a radical and uncompromising demand for your own liberation. They are the voice of your own wild, untamed divinity, roaring its way back into your conscious awareness.

Someone once sat across from me and said, “My dreams are a relentless horror show. I’m being chased, I’m falling, I’m screaming and no one can hear me.” I looked them in the eye and said, “Good. Your soul is finally turning up the volume on what your waking mind has been trying to ignore. The horror is not in the dream, my friend. The horror is in the life you have been living that is generating these dreams. Now, let’s talk about what’s really chasing you.” Harvard psychologist Deirdre Barrett, in her extensive research on dreams, has shown how they can be a source of profound insight and creative problem-solving. But their most vital function is emotional regulation. Dreams are where we metabolize our pain. They are where we digest the undigestible experiences of our lives. They are where we alchemize our leaden sorrows into golden wisdom. This is not a metaphor. It is a biological and spiritual reality. The alchemical fire of your dreams is literally re-wiring your neural pathways, creating new possibilities for healing and transformation. It is a nightly neuro-spiritual upgrade, if you have the courage to plug in.

Liberation is not about collecting ‘good karma’ for a better rebirth. Liberation is about freeing yourself from the weight of stored impressions altogether.

When Emotional Processing Goes Wrong

What happens when this process is disrupted? What happens when we don’t get enough REM sleep, or when the emotional content of our dreams is too overwhelming to process? This is when we see the emergence of nightmares, recurring dreams, and other sleep disturbances. These are not signs of a broken mind. They are the desperate cries of a soul that is drowning in unprocessed emotions. They are the smoke signals of a war being waged in the depths of your being. And you are not a helpless victim in this war. You are a warrior. And it is time for you to pick up your sword. The consequences of ignoring these signals are dire. Unprocessed emotions fester and rot, leading to anxiety, depression, and a host of physical ailments. Your life becomes a grey, muted landscape, devoid of passion and purpose.

Nightmares are often the result of trauma. They are the mind’s attempt to process an event that was too terrifying, too overwhelming to be integrated in the moment. The dream returns to the scene of the crime, again and again, not to torture you, but to try and make sense of what happened, to try and find a way to complete the story. It is a sacred and necessary pilgrimage to the site of your deepest wounds. And you do not have to make this journey alone. Your dreams are your allies. Your pain is your guide. And I am here to hold the lamp. But I will not shield you from the flames. I will not offer you false comfort or easy answers. I will simply stand with you in the fire, and remind you of your own strength to endure it.

Recurring dreams, on the other hand, often point to a persistent emotional conflict or an unresolved issue in our lives. The dream will keep repeating itself, with slight variations, until we get the message. It is a persistent and loving messenger, knocking on the door of our consciousness, asking us to pay attention to something we have been ignoring. And it will not give up. It will keep knocking, louder and louder, until you answer the goddamn door. And when you do, you may not like what you find on the other side. It may be a part of yourself you have long denied, a truth you have been avoiding, a choice you have been refusing to make. But I promise you, it will be the beginning of your freedom.

I've guided millions of people around the world through playful and insightful videos, courses, coaching, lectures, workshops, and retreats.

The Fierce Path to Emotional Freedom

So, what do we do with this information? How do we work with our dreams to facilitate our own healing? The first step is to stop running away. Stop pathologizing your pain. Stop trying to find a quick fix or a spiritual bypass for your discomfort. Your pain is not the problem. Your resistance to your pain is the problem. Your addiction to comfort is the problem. Your fear of your own power is the problem. The dream is just the mirror, reflecting it all back to you with ruthless compassion. And it will not flatter you. It will not coddle you. It will show you the unvarnished, often brutal, truth of who you are and what you have become.

Your dreams are inviting you into a deeper relationship with yourself. They are inviting you to feel what you have been refusing to feel. They are inviting you to embrace the full spectrum of your humanity, the light and the dark, the joy and the sorrow, the sacred and the profane.

This is not a path for the faint of heart. It is a fierce and often messy path. It requires courage, honesty, and a willingness to be uncomfortable. It requires you to turn towards your pain, to sit with it, to listen to its wisdom, and to allow it to transform you. It is the path of the spiritual warrior, the one who is willing to die to everything they are not in order to become everything they are. It is the path of fire and flood, of death and rebirth. And it is the only path worth walking. This path will cost you everything you thought you were. It will strip you bare of your illusions, your defenses, your precious identities. But in return, it will give you something infinitely more valuable: your own soul.

Head to the forest, pound the Earth, and cry or scream with abandon! You are a work in progress, and growth is a messy business.

Practical Steps for Deeper REM Sleep

While the inner work is paramount, there are also practical steps you can take to improve the quality of your REM sleep and support the process of emotional processing. This is not about bio-hacking your sleep for productivity. This is about creating the optimal conditions for your soul to do its sacred work. This is about building a temple for your own transformation. A temple where you can be safe enough to be dangerous, where you can be held enough to fall apart, where you can be loved enough to tell the truth. These are not just helpful hints. They are sacred obligations to your own being.

There is no other. No one is coming to save you. It's up to you.

  1. Create a Sanctuary for Sleep: Your bedroom should be a haven of peace and tranquility. Keep it dark, cool, and quiet. Remove all electronic devices, or at least turn them off well before bedtime. Let your bedroom be a place for rest, intimacy, and dreaming, and nothing else.

  2. Honor the Rhythms of Your Body: Go to bed and wake up at the same time every day, even on weekends. This helps to regulate your body’s internal clock and ensures that you are getting enough of all the different stages of sleep, including REM.

  3. Mindful Evenings: Avoid stimulants like caffeine and alcohol in the evening. These substances can interfere with the quality of your sleep and suppress REM. Instead, create a relaxing bedtime ritual. Take a warm bath, read a book of poetry, listen to calming music, or do some gentle stretching.

  4. Dream Journaling: Keep a journal by your bed and write down your dreams as soon as you wake up. Don’t try to analyze them right away. Just record them in as much detail as you can remember. This practice sends a clear message to your subconscious that you are listening, that you are ready to engage with its wisdom.

    This life is a profound chance to free your soul from bondage.

Find quality people and get to know them personally. Pay them what they’re worth, then give them the support they’ll need to hit home runs.

FAQ about REM Sleep and Emotional Processing

1. Can I have too much REM sleep?

While REM sleep is essential, an excess of it, particularly in the absence of deep sleep, can be associated with certain conditions like depression. The key is not to maximize one stage of sleep at the expense of others, but to foster a healthy and balanced sleep architecture. It’s about the symphony, not just a single instrument. It is about creating a life that is in harmony with the natural rhythms of your body and soul. A life where you work hard, play hard, and rest deep. A life where you are not constantly running on fumes, but are nourished from the deep well of your own being.

2. Are my dreams always symbolic?

Not all dreams are created equal. Some are simply the brain’s way of cleaning up the day’s debris. But many, especially the emotionally charged and recurring ones, are rich with symbolic meaning. The key is to approach them with curiosity and respect, to learn their unique language, rather than imposing a generic dream dictionary on them. Your dreams are not a puzzle to be solved. They are a love letter to be read. A love letter from your soul, to your soul. And it is written in a language that only you can understand. To try and interpret it with a generic dream dictionary is like trying to understand a Shakespearean sonnet by looking up the words in a technical manual. It is an act of profound disrespect to the artist.

3. What if I can’t remember my dreams?

Dream recall is a skill that can be cultivated. The intention to remember is the first and most important step. As you begin to pay more attention to your inner world, as you create a space for your dreams to be heard, you will find that they begin to speak to you more clearly. Be patient and persistent. But do not be passive. This is not a waiting game. It is a treasure hunt. And the treasure is you. And like any good treasure hunt, it will require you to decipher clues, to follow maps, to take risks, and to venture into the unknown. But the reward is immeasurable.

May you shed the constricting veils that imprison you, arrive at a profound clarity, and enjoy an unbridled authenticity.

The Fire Inside The Role of REM

Your dreams are not a problem to be solved. They are a mystery to be lived. They are a sacred fire that burns within you, a fire that purifies, that illuminates, and that transforms. Do not be afraid of its heat. Do not try to extinguish its flames. Your job is to tend to this fire, to honor its power, and to allow it to guide you home to the truth of who you are. This is not a gentle, flickering candle. This is a raging inferno. And it will burn away everything that is not true, everything that is not love, everything that is not you. It will leave you naked, raw, and utterly, terrifyingly free.

This is the work of a lifetime. It is the work of becoming a conscious and compassionate human being. It is the work of reclaiming your wholeness, your power, and your freedom. And it all begins in the dark, in the sacred and mysterious realm of your dreams. So I invite you, tonight, as you lay your head on your pillow, to whisper a prayer to the fierce and loving intelligence of your own being. A prayer of surrender. A prayer of gratitude. A prayer that says, ‘I am ready. Show me everything.’ And then, be prepared to be astonished by what is shown.

Pour that raw, unfiltered truth into a document. Don’t worry about sounding professional. Don’t worry about sounding polished. Sound human. Sound like someone who gives a damn.

faqs.question: Can meditation or spiritual practice change my dream patterns?
faqs.answer: Consistently, yes. Regular contemplative practice tends to produce dreams that are more vivid, more coherent, and more directly communicative. You are training your conscious mind to listen, and the unconscious responds by speaking more clearly.

faqs.question: How do I know if my dream about this topic is significant?
faqs.answer: Significance is not about the content — it is about the charge. If the dream stays with you, if it carries emotional weight, if you find yourself thinking about it hours later, it is significant. The unconscious does not waste energy on trivia.

faqs.question: What if I cannot remember my dreams at all?
faqs.answer: Dream recall is a skill, not a gift. Place a notebook by your bed. Set an intention before sleep. When you wake, do not move — stay in the position you woke in and let the images surface. Most people who say they do not dream simply have not trained the recall muscle.

phrasesUsed: I am love, you are love, it is all love.

There is no other. No one is coming to save you. It's up to you.
